chunk_summary: This section outlines induction therapy options for pediatric eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE), emphasizing individualized treatment based on disease severity, family capacity, and nutrition risk, with specific dosing and monitoring guidelines.
learning_objectives: Understand the three-step management process for pediatric EoE: induction, histologic response confirmation, and maintenance.
Identify and compare induction therapy options, including diet therapy, PPIs, and swallowed topical corticosteroids.
Learn dosing guidelines for PPIs and topical steroids in pediatric EoE treatment.
Recognize the importance of endoscopic monitoring and histologic assessment in therapy evaluation.
key_facts: Induction therapy options for EoE include diet therapy (empiric elimination or elemental diet), PPIs, and swallowed topical corticosteroids.
PPI dosing for pediatric EoE is weight-based, typically 1–2 mg/kg/day divided twice daily, with maximum doses up to 20–40 mg twice daily.
Swallowed fluticasone propionate dosing is age-stratified: 88–250 mg per dose (ages 1–3), 250–500 mg per dose (ages 4–10), and ~500 mg per dose (ages 11–18).
Endoscopic reassessment with biopsies is recommended after 8–12 weeks for elimination diets, topical steroids, or PPIs, and after at least 4 weeks for elemental diets.
Allergy testing is not reliable for identifying histologic triggers in EoE and should not guide food elimination.
reference_quote: Management of pediatric EoE requires three linked steps: induction therapy, objective confirmation of histologic response, and maintenance with periodic reassessment.
document_key_topics: Induction therapy options for pediatric EoE
Dosing guidelines for PPIs and topical steroids
Role of endoscopic monitoring in EoE management
Limitations of allergy testing in EoE treatment
